title: To amend the Immigration and Nationality Act to waive certain naturalization requirements for United States nationals, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This bill makes it easier for people who are U.S. nationals to become full U.S. citizens. U.S. nationals are people born in places like American Samoa who are connected to America but are not automatically citizens. The bill lets these people apply for citizenship without having to move to a U.S. state first, and they can skip requirements like English tests if they have lived in U.S. territories or states their whole lives. It also lets some children born to American parents in other countries become citizens by living in U.S. territories instead of having to come to the mainland United States.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Waiver of Certain Naturalization Requirements for United States Nationals to Become United States Citizens
sections.text:
Congress has found several important facts about United States nationals. United States nationals are people who have permanent loyalty to the United States, just like United States citizens do. These nationals serve in the military at very high rates compared to their population size. Some military officer positions and security clearances require people to be United States citizens, not just nationals. Many United States nationals want to become full United States citizens. These nationals attend schools that follow education rules approved by the United States Department of Education. United States nationals who serve in the military or other civilians who want to become citizens should be able to get citizenship quickly without having to move somewhere else, take more education tests, or pay any fees.

The law changes how certain United States nationals can become citizens. A person who is not a citizen but has permanent loyalty to the United States can become a citizen in two ways. First, if the person moves to live in any state, they can become a citizen by following the normal requirements. For these applications, living in the United States includes living in any territory that belongs to the United States. Second, if the person has lived continuously in any state or United States territory from birth until their citizenship application is approved, they can become a citizen without having to meet certain requirements about English language skills, civics knowledge, and some residence rules.

The Secretary of Homeland Security must make sure that citizenship applications, interviews, paperwork, oath ceremonies, and other citizenship processes are available in United States territories for three groups of people. These include people applying under the special rules for nationals who lived in territories from birth, people in the military applying for citizenship who live in territories, and certain children of citizens who live in territories.

When deciding if someone qualifies under the special rules for nationals, several things matter. If someone leaves any state or territory for more than one hundred eighty days in a row, this breaks their continuous residence unless they can prove they did not abandon their home during that time. The Secretary of Homeland Security can choose to skip the personal interview during the background check process. The Secretary can also choose to charge a lower fee for these special applications compared to regular citizenship applications, considering how much it costs to process each type of application.

The law also changes rules about children of United States nationals. A child can qualify for citizenship if they are temporarily in the United States legally and keeping that legal status, or if they live in a United States territory.
